summary: Italian Renaissance painter from Cremona. Most commonly identified by The Chess Game (depicting her sisters) and Boy Bitten by a Crawfish/Crab (which influenced Caravaggio). Court painter to Philip II of Spain. Student of Bernardino Campi. Most famous female painter of the Italian Renaissance.

works.name: The Chess Game (1555)
works.description: Depicts Anguissola's sisters Lucia, Minerva, and Europa playing chess while a maid observes. Europa laughs as Minerva raises her hand because Lucia is beating her. Signed 'virgin daughter of Amilcare.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Depicts her sisters Lucia, Minerva, and Europa playing chess; Europa laughs, Minerva raises her hand; maid observes
examples: young Europa laughs as Minerva puts up her hand in protest because Lucia is beating her at the titular board game
a maid observes three figures engaging in the title activity

clues.clue: Signed on a board: 'virgin daughter of Amilcare'
examples: the artist refers to herself as the "virgin daughter of Amilcare"
inscribed with this painter's name and the words, "virgin daughter of Amilcaris"

works.name: Boy Bitten by a Crawfish / Crab / Lobster
works.description: Chalk drawing of Anguissola's brother Asdrubale crying after being bitten by a crustacean. Influenced Caravaggio's Boy Bitten by a Lizard. Michelangelo was impressed by it.

clues.clue: Drawing of brother Asdrubale crying after being bitten by a crawfish/crab; influenced Caravaggio's Boy Bitten by a Lizard
examples: Caravaggio's painting Boy Bitten by a Lizard may have been inspired by a chalk sketch by this artist in which a child is bitten by a crustacean
Michelangelo's off-hand remark that it would be difficult to portray a weeping boy led this artist to create a charcoal sketch

clues.clue: Michelangelo was impressed when he saw the drawing
examples: Michaelangelo was supposedly deeply impressed with this artist's Boy Bitten by a Crayfish
Anguissola's talent was recognised when her drawing Boy Bitten by a Crayfish was seen by this Florentine painter

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Anguissola is identified and other clues.

clues.clue: Italian Renaissance painter from Cremona; most famous female painter of the Italian Renaissance
examples: likely the most famous female painter of the Italian Renaissance

clues.clue: Court painter to Philip II of Spain; arrived via recommendation of Duke of Alba; tutored Elizabeth of Valois
examples: became a court painter of Philip II of Spain after tutoring Elizabeth of Valois
Philip II tried to have this court painter married to a member of his court

clues.clue: Student of Bernardino Campi; self-portrait showing Campi painting a portrait of her
examples: included a self-portrait in a depiction of Bernardino Campi making a portrait of this artist

clues.clue: Anthony van Dyck visited her when she was 80/92 years old and painted her portrait
examples: A burgeoning Anthony van Dyck learnt many of his portrait techniques when he visited this then-80 year old artist

clues.clue: Self-portrait miniature with green medallion featuring monogram of sisters' names
examples: a large green medallion embossed with a monogram mixing all of the first letters of her sisters' names

clues.clue: Joseph Cornell shadowbox contains sepia copy of one of her portraits
examples: In a Joseph Cornell shadowbox, a sepia-toned copy of one of this artist's portraits

clues.clue: Family: father Amilcare, brother Asdrubale, sisters Lucia, Minerva, Europa
examples: Asdrubale holds the wrist of his father Amilcare in a portrait of this family

comprehensive_summary:
Sofonisba Anguissola was an Italian Renaissance painter from Cremona, considered the most famous female painter of the Italian Renaissance. Her best-known work, The Chess Game (1555), depicts her sisters Lucia, Minerva, and Europa playing chess while a maid observes. Europa laughs as Minerva raises her hand because Lucia is winning. It is signed 'virgin daughter of Amilcare' on a board.

Her chalk drawing Boy Bitten by a Crawfish shows her brother Asdrubale crying after being bitten by a crustacean. Michelangelo was reportedly impressed by it, and according to Roberto Longhi, it influenced Caravaggio's Boy Bitten by a Lizard. She was a student of Bernardino Campi and created a self-portrait showing Campi painting her.

Anguissola served as court painter to Philip II of Spain, arriving via recommendation of the Duke of Alba and tutoring Elizabeth of Valois. Anthony van Dyck visited her when she was around 92 years old and painted her portrait. Her self-portrait miniature features a green medallion with a monogram of her sisters' initials. A Joseph Cornell shadowbox incorporates a copy of one of her portraits.

cards.front: Painting: depicts three sisters playing chess as a younger one laughs and a maid observes; signed 'virgin daughter of Amilcare'
Painting: drawing of a boy (Asdrubale) crying after being bitten by a crawfish; influenced Caravaggio's Boy Bitten by a Lizard; impressed Michelangelo
